ac:rich-text-body.p: SKA-Low uses a hierarchical array architecture in which 512 stations are distributed across the Western Australian landscape. Each station is itself subdivided into substations, which are phased-array groupings of antennas that generate independent, steerable beams.

ul.li: Substations within each station: Groups of antennas (typically 16–64 dipoles per substation) that can be independently phased to form coherent beams

p: This hierarchical structure provides flexibility in beamforming and allows substations to be configured for different science goals within the same station footprint.
A substation is a logical and/or physical grouping of antennas within a station that shares front-end electronics and can form independent phased-array beams. Key characteristics:

ul.li: Phased-array beamforming: Antennas within a substation have their signals combined with controlled phase delays, allowing the beam to be steered in any direction within a wide field of view (~30–50 degrees depending on frequency)
Independent beams: Each substation can form one primary beam. Multiple substations within a station can simultaneously form beams in different directions.
Tied-array mode: Substations can combine their outputs coherently for ultra-high sensitivity observations (see Tied-Array Beams section)
Station-level combination: Outputs from all substations within a station are coherently combined to form the station's contribution to the full interferometer

ol.li: Signal capture: All 256 dipoles in a station receive the sky signal
Grouping: Dipoles are assigned to substations (e.g., four substations of 64 dipoles each, or eight substations of 32 dipoles each)

Phasing: Each substation applies frequency-dependent phase corrections to align signals from its antennas in a chosen direction. The phase pattern is updated in real-time to track a target or steer across the sky.
Beam combination: Each substation's phased output is digitized and sent to the correlator/beamformer back-end
Station synthesis: The correlator coherently combines all substation beams from a station, treating each substation as a virtual "antenna" in the interferometer

p: The result is a station-level beam that reflects the combined sensitivity of all 256 dipoles but with flexibility to partially steer different substation beams within the station's instantaneous field of view.
Each station acts as a phased-array antenna in the SKA-Low interferometer. The contribution of each station is:

li.strong: Formed by combining outputs from its substations

ul.li: Pointed at a common direction (or multiple directions via commensals)

Characterized by a station beam pattern with:
Primary beam (main lobe) ~10 degrees diameter at 50 MHz, narrowing to ~1 degree at 350 MHz
Sidelobe patterns determined by substation geometry

p: The full SKA-Low image is formed by correlating signals from all 512 station beams. This two-level hierarchy (substations → stations → interferometer) provides computational efficiency and operational flexibility.

tr.td: Seconds to minutes (depends on ionospheric conditions)

p: Flexibility: Substation partitioning allows observers to tailor array performance to their science needs:

ul.li: Many substations (small size): High-resolution beams, large instantaneous field of view, good for survey observations and wide-field monitoring
Few substations (large size): Lower computation overhead, simplified phasing, good for deep, narrow-field observations

p: Pulsar timing and search: Tied-array modes combine substations coherently for maximum sensitivity to pulsars and other point sources.
Calibration: Substation geometry directly impacts the station beam pattern and must be precisely characterized for accurate calibration and image reconstruction.
When planning SKA-Low observations, you may specify:

ul.li: Desired substation configuration: Number and size of substations (subject to hardware constraints and operational feasibility)
Beamforming mode: Tied-array (maximum coherence), phased-array (flexible steering), or hybrid modes
Field of view requirement: Determines the smallest viable substation size

p: The SKA commissioning team and User Support will advise on achievable configurations during proposal development.

ac:rich-text-body.p: At SKA-Low frequencies (50–350 MHz), the ionosphere introduces phase distortions that degrade coherence over timescales of seconds to minutes. Real-time phase correction (using calibration sources) is essential for maintaining beam quality and coherence in tied-array and correlator modes. Observers should budget observing time for calibration cycles.

p: The SKA-Low substation architecture is designed for future upgrades:

ul.li: Digital back-ends: Firmware updates can enable new beamforming modes and configurations without hardware changes
Receiver improvements: Substation receiver chains can be enhanced over time to reduce noise and extend frequency coverage
Computational upgrades: Additional processing capacity allows more simultaneous substation beams or higher data rates

p: Proposals can request new modes during early operational cycles; feasibility will be evaluated by the commissioning team.

ac:rich-text-body.p: For detailed technical specifications of substations, see the SKA System Specifications documents. For questions about substation configurations for your science, contact the SKA User Support team.
